steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The first sentence claims Chaucer pioneered the literary use of English. The second sentence (containing the blank) provides concrete evidence for that claim: his manuscripts contain the first documented uses of over 2,000 English words, earning him the title "the first finder of our fair language." The transition must signal that the second sentence supports and strengthens the first.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Besides,: "Besides" adds a separate, supplementary point rather than reinforcing the main claim. The second sentence is direct evidence, not a separate bonus fact. ✗
  • Choice B — However,: "However" signals contrast or contradiction. The second sentence does not contradict the claim that Chaucer pioneered English literature — it proves it. ✗
  • Choice C — On the contrary,: "On the contrary" signals a reversal of what was just said. The second sentence goes in the same direction as the first, not the opposite direction. ✗
  • Choice D — In fact,: "In fact" introduces evidence that confirms and intensifies a preceding claim. Here it signals: the pioneer claim is true, and here is the documented proof. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
